input.background: A case-control study examined the relationship between cellular telephone use and intracranial tumors. The study (completed between 1994 and 1998) included 782 cases with various types of intracranial tumors and 799 controls with a variety of nonmalignant conditions. Subjects were considered to be "exposed" if they reported cellular telephone use for more than 100 hours. The odds ratios (and $95 \%$ confidence intervals) for various tumor types were: for glioma 0.9 ( 0.5 to 1.6 ), for meningioma 0.7 ( 0.3 to 1.7 ), for acoustic neuroma 1.4 ( 0.6 to 3.5 ), for all tumor types combined 1.0 ( 0.6 to 1.5 ).
input.question: Do results support or fail to support the theory that handheld cellular telephone use causes brain tumors?

output.answer: The point estimate for all tumor types combined is 1.0 indicating no association between recent cell phone use and intracranial tumors in general. All confidence interval are consistent with population odds ratios of 1 (no association).

cellular_telephone_use.description: Whether subjects reported cellular telephone use for more than 100 hours.

tumor_type.description: The type of intracranial tumor diagnosed in subjects.

odds_ratio.description: The odds ratio for cellular telephone use associated with various tumor types.

confidence_interval.description: The 95% confidence intervals for the odds ratios of cellular telephone use associated with various tumor types.

case_control_status.description: Indicates whether the subject was a case (intracranial tumor) or a control (nonmalignant condition).

meta_info.caption: Cell phones and brain tumors, study 1.
